---
pagetitle: "About | Above .500"
---

```{=html}
<span class="kicker">About</span>
<h1 class="headline">What is Above .500?</h1>
<p class="dek">A one-person attempt to keep the spirit of data-driven sports
forecasting alive after FiveThirtyEight shut down.</p>

<div class="section-head"><h2>The idea</h2></div>
```

I'm [George Pu](https://github.com/GeorgeRPu), and I grew up refreshing
FiveThirtyEight's forecast pages every morning during the NBA season. When 538
went away in 2023, I lost my favorite source of transparent, model-driven
sports predictions — so I decided to build my own.

Above .500 is that project. Every forecast on this site comes from a
statistical model I wrote, using publicly available data and open methods. No
proprietary feeds, no black boxes.

```{=html}
<div class="section-head"><h2>How it works</h2></div>
```

The models are Python code that run every time the site is built. A nightly
GitHub Action re-renders everything, so the numbers you see are never more than
a day old. There are no manually updated spreadsheets or stale JSON files — if
the upstream data has changed, the next build picks it up.

Each forecast page explains its own methodology. If you want to dig deeper,
the full source is on [GitHub](https://github.com/GeorgeRPu/above-five-hundred).

```{=html}
<div class="section-head"><h2>Why "Above .500"?</h2></div>
```

In sports, a .500 record means you're perfectly average — you win as often as
you lose. "Above .500" is the goal for any team that wants to make the
playoffs. It felt like the right name for a site trying to do better than a
coin flip.
